sentences.target: Mia is a student.
sentences.en: Mia is a student.

sentences.target: She lives in a small town.
sentences.en: She lives in a small town.

sentences.target: Every morning she wakes up early.
sentences.en: Every morning she wakes up early.

sentences.target: She drinks tea and eats bread.
sentences.en: She drinks tea and eats bread.

sentences.target: Then she walks to school.
sentences.en: Then she walks to school.

sentences.target: Her school is near the park.
sentences.en: Her school is near the park.

sentences.target: She likes her teacher very much.
sentences.en: She likes her teacher very much.

sentences.target: In the afternoon she reads books.
sentences.en: In the afternoon she reads books.

sentences.target: She also plays with her dog.
sentences.en: She also plays with her dog.

sentences.target: In the evening the family eats together.
sentences.en: In the evening the family eats together.

sentences.target: Mia is happy in her small town.
sentences.en: Mia is happy in her small town.

wakes.note: wake up means to stop sleeping.

culturalNote.body: In many English-speaking countries, small towns are valued for a slower pace of life, where people often know their neighbors and daily routines center on family, school and local shops.

questions.explanation: The story begins, "Mia is a student."

questions.stem: What does Mia do in the afternoon?

sentences.target: My name is Tom.
sentences.en: My name is Tom.

sentences.target: I have a big family.
sentences.en: I have a big family.

sentences.target: My mother is a doctor.
sentences.en: My mother is a doctor.

sentences.target: My father works in a shop.
sentences.en: My father works in a shop.

sentences.target: I have one sister and one brother.
sentences.en: I have one sister and one brother.

sentences.target: My sister is older than me.
sentences.en: My sister is older than me.

sentences.target: My brother is only five years old.
sentences.en: My brother is only five years old.

sentences.target: We live in a house with a garden.
sentences.en: We live in a house with a garden.

sentences.target: On Sunday we visit my grandmother.
sentences.en: On Sunday we visit my grandmother.

sentences.target: She makes a cake for everyone.
sentences.en: She makes a cake for everyone.

sentences.target: I love my family very much.
sentences.en: I love my family very much.

older.note: older than means greater in age.

culturalNote.body: Sunday is traditionally a day for family in much of the English-speaking world. Many families gather for a shared meal, and visiting grandparents is a common weekend custom.

questions.explanation: "My brother is only five years old."

questions.stem: Who does the family visit on Sunday?

sentences.target: On Saturday morning Anna went to the market.
sentences.en: On Saturday morning Anna went to the market.

sentences.target: She wanted to buy fresh vegetables and fruit.
sentences.en: She wanted to buy fresh vegetables and fruit.

sentences.target: The market was busy and full of people.
sentences.en: The market was busy and full of people.

sentences.target: First she stopped at the vegetable stall.
sentences.en: First she stopped at the vegetable stall.

sentences.target: "How much are the tomatoes?" she asked.
sentences.en: "How much are the tomatoes?" she asked.

sentences.target: "Two pounds a kilo," the seller answered.
sentences.en: "Two pounds a kilo," the seller answered.

sentences.target: Anna bought tomatoes, onions and a lettuce.
sentences.en: Anna bought tomatoes, onions and a lettuce.

sentences.target: Next she walked to the bakery.
sentences.en: Next she walked to the bakery.

sentences.target: The smell of fresh bread was wonderful.
sentences.en: The smell of fresh bread was wonderful.

sentences.target: She chose a loaf of brown bread and some cheese.
sentences.en: She chose a loaf of brown bread and some cheese.

sentences.target: Before she left, she bought flowers for the table.
sentences.en: Before she left, she bought flowers for the table.

sentences.target: Her bag was heavy, but she felt happy.
sentences.en: Her bag was heavy, but she felt happy.

sentences.target: She walked home slowly in the warm sun.
sentences.en: She walked home slowly in the warm sun.

sentences.target: That evening she cooked a big dinner for her friends.
sentences.en: That evening she cooked a big dinner for her friends.

much.note: how much asks about price or quantity.

loaf.note: a loaf is one whole baked piece of bread.

culturalNote.body: Open-air and covered markets remain popular across Britain and Ireland. Many towns hold a weekly market day where locals buy fresh produce, bread and flowers directly from sellers.

sentences.target: It was Friday, and Sam was tired after a long week.
sentences.en: It was Friday, and Sam was tired after a long week.

sentences.target: He called his friend Leo on the phone.
sentences.en: He called his friend Leo on the phone.

sentences.target: "What shall we do this weekend?" he asked.
sentences.en: "What shall we do this weekend?" he asked.

sentences.target: Leo had a good idea.
sentences.en: Leo had a good idea.

sentences.target: "Let's go to the mountains and walk," he said.
sentences.en: "Let's go to the mountains and walk," he said.

sentences.target: On Saturday morning they took the early train.
sentences.en: On Saturday morning they took the early train.

sentences.target: The weather was cold but sunny.
sentences.en: The weather was cold but sunny.

sentences.target: They walked for three hours along a green path.
sentences.en: They walked for three hours along a green path.

sentences.target: At the top of the hill they ate their sandwiches.
sentences.en: At the top of the hill they ate their sandwiches.

sentences.target: The view over the valley was beautiful.
sentences.en: The view over the valley was beautiful.

sentences.target: In the afternoon it began to rain.
sentences.en: In the afternoon it began to rain.

sentences.target: They ran to a small café to stay dry.
sentences.en: They ran to a small café to stay dry.

sentences.target: They drank hot coffee and laughed about the day.
sentences.en: They drank hot coffee and laughed about the day.

sentences.target: It was a tiring weekend, but a great one.
sentences.en: It was a tiring weekend, but a great one.

shall.note: shall we...? suggests doing something together.

culturalNote.body: Walking in the hills and mountains is a much-loved weekend pastime in Britain and Ireland. Even in changeable weather, people set out with sandwiches and a flask, often ending the day in a warm café or pub.

sentences.target: Dear Maria, I have finally arrived in London.
sentences.en: Dear Maria, I have finally arrived in London.

sentences.target: The journey was long, but everything went smoothly.
sentences.en: The journey was long, but everything went smoothly.

sentences.target: The city is even busier than I had imagined.
sentences.en: The city is even busier than I had imagined.

sentences.target: On my first day, I simply walked around the center.
sentences.en: On my first day, I simply walked around the center.

sentences.target: I crossed the river on a beautiful old bridge.
sentences.en: I crossed the river on a beautiful old bridge.

sentences.target: Although it was raining, I did not mind at all.
sentences.en: Although it was raining, I did not mind at all.

sentences.target: Yesterday I visited a famous museum near the station.
sentences.en: Yesterday I visited a famous museum near the station.

sentences.target: The entrance was free, which surprised me.
sentences.en: The entrance was free, which surprised me.

sentences.target: I spent the whole afternoon looking at paintings.
sentences.en: I spent the whole afternoon looking at paintings.

sentences.target: Afterwards, I found a quiet café and wrote in my notebook.
sentences.en: Afterwards, I found a quiet café and wrote in my notebook.

sentences.target: The people here have been friendly and helpful.
sentences.en: The people here have been friendly and helpful.

sentences.target: When I asked for directions, a stranger walked me to the corner.
sentences.en: When I asked for directions, a stranger walked me to the corner.

sentences.target: Tomorrow I plan to take a train to the coast.
sentences.en: Tomorrow I plan to take a train to the coast.

sentences.target: I have heard that the cliffs there are spectacular.
sentences.en: I have heard that the cliffs there are spectacular.

sentences.target: If you have time next summer, you really should come.
sentences.en: If you have time next summer, you really should come.

sentences.target: I would love to show you all my favorite places.
sentences.en: I would love to show you all my favorite places.

sentences.target: Take care of yourself, and write back soon.
sentences.en: Take care of yourself, and write back soon.

sentences.target: With love, Daniel.
sentences.en: With love, Daniel.

mind.note: not mind means not to be bothered.

directions.note: ask for directions = ask the way.

culturalNote.body: Many of the United Kingdom's national museums and galleries are free to enter, including the British Museum and the National Gallery in London. Visitors can spend hours among world-famous collections without paying admission.

sentences.target: Sofia had been looking for a job for several months.
sentences.en: Sofia had been looking for a job for several months.

sentences.target: At last, a small company offered her a position.
sentences.en: At last, a small company offered her a position.

sentences.target: On her first morning, she felt both excited and nervous.
sentences.en: On her first morning, she felt both excited and nervous.

sentences.target: She arrived ten minutes early to make a good impression.
sentences.en: She arrived ten minutes early to make a good impression.

sentences.target: A friendly colleague met her at the entrance.
sentences.en: A friendly colleague met her at the entrance.

sentences.target: He showed her around the office and introduced the team.
sentences.en: He showed her around the office and introduced the team.

sentences.target: Everyone seemed welcoming, which helped her to relax.
sentences.en: Everyone seemed welcoming, which helped her to relax.

sentences.target: Her manager explained the main tasks for the week.
sentences.en: Her manager explained the main tasks for the week.

sentences.target: At first, the new software was difficult to understand.
sentences.en: At first, the new software was difficult to understand.

sentences.target: However, she asked questions and slowly learned the system.
sentences.en: However, she asked questions and slowly learned the system.

sentences.target: During lunch, she chatted with her colleagues in the kitchen.
sentences.en: During lunch, she chatted with her colleagues in the kitchen.

sentences.target: They gave her useful advice about the company.
sentences.en: They gave her useful advice about the company.

sentences.target: By the afternoon, she already felt more confident.
sentences.en: By the afternoon, she already felt more confident.

sentences.target: She finished her first report just before five o'clock.
sentences.en: She finished her first report just before five o'clock.

sentences.target: On the way home, she smiled to herself.
sentences.en: On the way home, she smiled to herself.

sentences.target: The day had gone far better than she had expected.
sentences.en: The day had gone far better than she had expected.

sentences.target: She knew there would be challenges ahead.
sentences.en: She knew there would be challenges ahead.

sentences.target: But for now, she was proud of how she had begun.
sentences.en: But for now, she was proud of how she had begun.

make.note: make a good impression = appear positively.

by.note: by the afternoon = no later than.

way.note: on the way home = while going home.

culturalNote.body: In many British and Irish workplaces, a new employee's first day includes an informal tour, introductions to the team and a relaxed chat over tea or lunch. Asking questions is seen as a sign of engagement, not weakness.
